messages.content:
(32 words max) Explain the quantitative
relationship between the
elemental composition by
mass and the empirical
formula of a pure substance.

The quantitative relationship between the elemental composition by mass and the empirical formula of a pure substance is that the mass of each element in the compound is in proportion to the ratio of their atomic masses as determined by the empirical formula.

(32 words max) Explain the quantitative
relationship between the
mass spectrum of an element
and the masses of the
element’s isotopes.
The mass spectrum of an element shows the abundance of isotopes present and their masses, allowing for the determination of the average atomic mass based on their relative abundances.

(32 words max) Explain the quantitative
relationship between the
elemental composition by
mass and the composition of
substances in a mixture.

The elemental composition by mass refers to the percentage of each element in a pure substance, while the composition of substances in a mixture refers to the relative amounts of different substances present.

(32 words max) Represent the electron
configuration of an element
or ions of an element using
the Aufbau principle.

The Aufbau principle dictates that electrons fill atomic orbitals starting with the lowest energy levels first, following the order of increasing atomic number and adhering to the Pauli exclusion principle.

(32 words max)  Explain the relationship
between the photoelectron
spectrum of an atom or
ion and:
a. The electron configuration
of the species.
b. The interactions
between the electrons
and the nucleus.

a. The photoelectron spectrum reveals energy levels of electrons, allowing determination of electron configuration.
b. The spectrum shows energy absorbed by electrons to break free from the nucleus, indicating interactions between electrons and nucleus.

(32 words max) Explain the relationship
between trends in atomic
properties of elements and
electronic structure
and periodicity.

Trends in atomic properties, such as atomic radius or ionization energy, are determined by the arrangement of electrons in the elements' electronic structure. This relationship follows periodic patterns known as periodicity.

(32 words max) Explain the relationship
between trends in the
reactivity of elements
and periodicity

The reactivity of elements follows trends based on periodicity, with elements in the same group having similar reactivity due to their similar outer electron configurations and tendencies to gain, lose, or share electrons.

(32 words max) Explain the relationship
between the type of bonding
and the properties of the
elements participating in
the bond.
The type of bonding, whether it is ionic, covalent, or metallic, influences the properties of elements by affecting factors such as melting point, solubility, conductivity, and strength of the bond.

(32 words max) Represent the relationship
between potential energy
and distance between
atoms, based on factors that
influence the interaction
strength.
The potential energy between atoms is inversely related to distance, meaning as they come closer, potential energy decreases due to attractive forces like ionic, covalent, or metallic bonding.

(32 words max) Represent an ionic solid with
a particulate model that is
consistent with Coulomb’s
law and the properties of the
constituent ions.

In an ionic solid, positively and negatively charged ions are arranged in a lattice structure, held together by strong electrostatic attractions based on Coulomb's law, resulting in high melting points and conductivity when molten or dissolved.

(32 words max)  Represent a metallic
solid and/or alloy using a
model to show essential
characteristics of the
structure and interactions
present in the substance.

In a metallic solid, metal atoms are arranged in a close-packed crystal lattice structure held together by a sea of delocalized electrons that contribute to high electrical conductivity and malleability.

(32 words max)  Represent a molecule with a
Lewis diagram.
A Lewis diagram represents a molecule by showing the arrangement of atoms and valence electrons in a simple, two-dimensional format, indicating the bonding and lone pairs of electrons.

what are the keywords: TOPIC 1.1
Moles and
Molar Mass
SUGGESTED SKILL
Mathematical
Routines
5.B
Identify an appropriate
theory, definition, or
mathematical relationship
to solve a problem.
AVAILABLE RESOURCES
§ Classroom Resource >
Quantitative Skills in
the AP Sciences
§ AP Chemistry Lab
Manual > Investigation
3: What Makes Hard
Water Hard?
§ Classroom Resource >
Guided Inquiry
Activities for the
Classroom: Lesson 1 LEARNING OBJECTIVE
SPQ-1.A
Calculate quantities of a
substance or its relative
number of particles using
dimensional analysis and the
mole concept.
ESSENTIAL KNOWLEDGE
SPQ-1.A.1
One cannot count particles directly while
performing laboratory work. Thus, there must
be a connection between the masses of
substances reacting and the actual number of
particles undergoing chemical changes.
SPQ-1.A.2
Avogadro’s number (N = 6.022 × 1023 mol−1
A
)
provides the connection between the number
of moles in a pure sample of a substance and
the number of constituent particles (or formula
units) of that substance.
SPQ-1.A.3
Expressing the mass of an individual atom
or molecule in atomic mass units (amu) is
useful because the average mass in amu of
one particle (atom or molecule) or formula
unit of a substance will always be numerically
equal to the molar mass of that substance
in grams. Thus, there is a quantitative
connection between the mass of a substance
and the number of particles that the
substance contains.
EQN: n = m/M
Keywords: Moles, Molar Mass, Mathematical Routines, Theory, Definition, Mathematical Relationship, Solve a Problem, Avogadro's Number, Molar Mass, Particles, Dimensional Analysis.

what are the keywords: TOPIC 1.2
Mass Spectroscopy
of Elements
LEARNING OBJECTIVE
SPQ-1.B
Explain the quantitative
relationship between the
mass spectrum of an element
and the masses of the
element’s isotopes.
ESSENTIAL KNOWLEDGE
SPQ-1.B.1
The mass spectrum of a sample containing a
single element can be used to determine the
identity of the isotopes of that element and the
relative abundance of each isotope in nature.
SPQ-1.B.2
The average atomic mass of an element can
be estimated from the weighted average of
the isotopic masses using the mass of each
isotope and its relative abundance.
 INTERPRETING MASS SPECTRA
Interpreting mass spectra of samples
containing multiple elements or peaks arising
from species other than singly charged
monatomic ions will not be assessed on
the AP Exam.
ENDURING UNDERSTANDING
SPQ-1
The mole allows different units to be compared.
Keywords: Mass Spectroscopy, Elements, Mass Spectrum, Isotopes, Quantitative Relationship, Relative Abundance, Average Atomic Mass, Weighted Average, Interpretation, Mole, Comparisons.

what are the keywords:  TOPIC 1.3
Elemental
Composition of
Pure Substances
LEARNING OBJECTIVE
SPQ-2.A
Explain the quantitative
relationship between the
elemental composition by
mass and the empirical
formula of a pure substance.
ESSENTIAL KNOWLEDGE
SPQ-2.A.1
Some pure substances are composed of
individual molecules, while others consist
of atoms or ions held together in fixed
proportions as described by a formula unit.
SPQ-2.A.2
According to the law of definite proportions,
the ratio of the masses of the constituent
elements in any pure sample of that compound
is always the same.
SPQ-2.A.3
The chemical formula that lists the lowest
whole number ratio of atoms of the elements in
a compound is the empirical formula.
ENDURING UNDERSTANDING
SPQ-2
Chemical formulas identify substances by their unique combination of atoms.

Keywords: Elemental Composition, Pure Substances, Quantitative Relationship, Mass, Empirical Formula, Molecules, Atoms, Ions, Law of Definite Proportions, Chemical Formula, Empirical Formula, Unique Combination of Atoms.

what are the keywords: TOPIC 1.4
Composition
of Mixtures
Required Course Content
LEARNING OBJECTIVE
SPQ-2.B
Explain the quantitative
relationship between the
elemental composition by
mass and the composition of
substances in a mixture.
ESSENTIAL KNOWLEDGE
SPQ-2.B.1
While pure substances contain molecules or
formula units of a single type, mixtures contain
molecules or formula units of two or more
types, whose relative proportions can vary.
SPQ-2.B.2
Elemental analysis can be used to determine
the relative numbers of atoms in a substance
and to determine its purity.
ENDURING UNDERSTANDING
SPQ-2
Chemical formulas identify substances by their unique combination of atoms.
Keywords: Composition, Mixtures, Quantitative Relationship, Elemental Composition, Elements, Mass, Substances, Types, Variability, Relative Proportions, Elemental Analysis, Purity, Chemical Formulas.
